coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots based multicolor fluorescence sensor for ratiometric and colorimetric dual-model detection of Cu2+
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Gengli
Luo, Xueli
Lin, Wanmei
Tang, Wenzhi
Yue, Tianli
Wang, Jianlong
Li, Zhonghong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a multicolor fluorescence sensor with two emission peaks at 445 nm (F445) and 560 nm (F560) under excitation wavelength of 365 nm was facilely fabricated by hybridizing blue and yellow fluorescence carbon dots (B-CDs and Y-CDs). Cu2+ could selectively coordinate with the aromatic amino groups of the Y-CDs, thus forming the Cu2+-Y-CDs complex through electron pair sharing to efficiently quench the fluorescence of B-CDs by Förster resonance energy transfer. Meanwhile, the complex could suppress photoinduced electron transfer thus enhancing the fluorescence of Y-CDs. The unique interrelated opposite responses of the sensor toward Cu2+ could generate a remarkable change in the value of F560/F445 and consequently give rise to more easily distinguishable fluorescent color tonality changes from blue to yellow for colorimetric detection. The sensor possessed a low detection limit of 44.05 nmol L−1 and 169.46 nmol L−1 to recognize Cu2+ by the ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection, respectively. Recoveries of 96.8–105.9% (RSD <3.8%) and 94.4–108.5% (RSD <7.7%) were respectively obtained in spiked samples by the ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection, indicating their promising potentials to quick pre-warning of Cu2+ for ensuring food safety and improving public health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent nanomaterials
Ratiometric fluorescence
Colorimetric assay
Heavy metal
Smartphone
Portable device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric detection of Cu2+ with smartphone
Determination of Cu2+ in real samples
Colorimetric detection of Cu2+ with smartphone
Determination of Cu2+ in spiked samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of Y-CDs (A) and B-CDs (D), the inset was the size distribution histogram and the HRTEM image with lattice parameter; UV–vis spectrum (blue line) and fluorescence emission spectrum (pink line) of Y-CDs (B) and B-CDs (E), the insets of (a) and (b) were the absence and presence of 365 nm UV light; Fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs (C) and B-CDs (F) at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence spectra for the sensor with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the inset: the corresponding images under UV lamp (365 nm); (B) Plot of F560/F445 vs Cu2+ concentrations; (C) CIE chromaticity diagram of the sensor with different Cu2+ concentration; (D) F560/F445 of the sensor in presence of different metal ions (blue bars) and their co-existence with Cu2+ (pink bars) (Co2+, Fe3+ and Hg2+: 100 μmol L−1, other: 200 μmol L−1, Cu2+: 20 μmol L−1), the inset images showed the sensor solutions in the presence of various metal ions under 365 nm UV light.

(A) The relationship between B/G and Cu2+ concentration (0–45 μmol L−1), the insets were the corresponding fluorescence photographs captured by the smartphone camera; (B) Plot of B/G vs Cu2+ concentrations (0–25 μmol L−1).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and Y-CDs before and after addition of Cu2+ (20 μmol L−1); (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of the Y-CDs versus various concentrations of Cu2+ (0–45 μmol L−1), the inset were the corresponding photograph under ambient light.; (C) Fluorescence spectrum of B-CDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Y-CDs + Cu2+; (D) Fluorescence decays curve of the B-CDs in the mixture before and after introduction of Cu2+.
Carbon dots based multicolor fluorescence sensor for the ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection of Cu2+.
Detection of Cu2+ in spiked samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots based multicolor fluorescence sensor for ratiometric and colorimetric dual-model detection of Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (YangLing), Ministry of Agriculture, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (YangLing)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (YangLing), Ministry of Agriculture, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China.steveli@nwsuaf.edu.cn

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a multicolor fluorescence sensor with two emission peaks at 445 nm (F445) and 560 nm (F560) under excitation wavelength of 365 nm was facilely fabricated by hybridizing blue and yellow fluorescence carbon dots (B-CDs and Y-CDs). Cu2+ could selectively coordinate with the aromatic amino groups of the Y-CDs, thus forming the Cu2+-Y-CDs complex through electron pair sharing to efficiently quench the fluorescence of B-CDs by Förster resonance energy transfer. Meanwhile, the complex could suppress photoinduced electron transfer thus enhancing the fluorescence of Y-CDs. The unique interrelated opposite responses of the sensor toward Cu2+ could generate a remarkable change in the value of F560/F445 and consequently give rise to more easily distinguishable fluorescent color tonality changes from blue to yellow for colorimetric detection. The sensor possessed a low detection limit of 44.05 nmol L−1 and 169.46 nmol L−1 to recognize Cu2+ by the ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection, respectively. Recoveries of 96.8–105.9% (RSD <3.8%) and 94.4–108.5% (RSD <7.7%) were respectively obtained in spiked samples by the ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection, indicating their promising potentials to quick pre-warning of Cu2+ for ensuring food safety and improving public health.

ce:section.ce:para: As the third trace metal element in human body, the copper ions (Cu2+) plays significant functions such as a catalytic cofactor of many enzymes (e.g. lysyl oxidase and Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase) [1–4]. However, excess Cu2+ in human body would cause some severe diseases such as Wilson's disease and neurodegenerative disorders [5,6]. The main source of Cu2+ for human body is the uptake from oral intake of copper-containing food [3]. The development of modern agriculture and industry has leaded to excessive Cu2+ contamination in environments that can naturally transfer to food and threaten human health [5]. Hence, accurate and sensitive detection of Cu2+ in food samples is particularly significant. Until now, different analytical techniques have been applied for detecting Cu2+ including flame atomic absorption spectroscopy (FAAS) [7], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [8] and electrochemical method [9]. Despite these methods were featured with accurate and convincing results, the expensive equipment and professional operator still restrain their widespread applications in practical fields. Therefore, more convenient and facile strategies are indispensable for detecting Cu2+.

Fluorescence analysis is suitable for detection application for its excellent sensitivity, rapid response and availability of a wide range of fluorophores including quantum dots [10], metal organic framework [11], nanoclusters [12] and carbon dots (CDs) [13,14]. CDs were widely explored for fluorescence analysis because of their excellent biocompatibility, tunable optical and good water solubility [15,16]. For example, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was selected as precursor to prepare single emission (long wavelength) CDs for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ because it possessed N-riched functional groups (e.g.-NH2) that could effectively capture Cu2+ [14]. However, the absolute intensity-dependent sensor for the single emission fluorescence detection (turn-off or turn-on) was easily vulnerable to probe concentration, laboratory instruments parameters and excitation intensity [17], which could result in fluctuation of their absolute signal intensity and thus make the results less accurate. Ratiometric fluorescence sensor with built-in self-calibration property was applied to minimize nonspecific influences by calculating the ratio of two well resolved emission peak intensities, thus providing more accurate and reliable results [18,19]. Two main strategies have been developed for constructing ratiometric fluorescence sensor including synthesizing single intrinsic dual-emission emitters [20,21] and hybridizing multiple emitters together either chemically (bonded) or physically (mixed) [22–26]. Hybridizing multiple emitters has become one of the most effective methods for constructing ratiometric fluorescence sensor because there is no need for elaborate molecular design and sophisticated synthesis [19]. Generally, for a dual emitter sensor, one of emitters was utilized as a signal report and the other as a reference signal [4,25]. To date, few ratiometric fluorescence probes with opposite response toward analytes have been reported, which could provide a remarkable change in the ratio of two fluorescence signals and thus makes the sensor more sensitive for the analytes [19]. Hence, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ detection could be prepared by hybridizing OPD based CDs as the sensitive emitter with other CDs with well-resolved fluorescence emission.

Moreover, compared with the color intensity changes (i.e., color brightness) bring from the single emission, the fluorescent color tonality (i.e., color hue) of ratiometric fluorescence sensor could change more easily recognizable by human eye, which possessed promising potentials for colorimetric analysis of analytes [25,27]. However, the method by naked eye could only obtain the approximate concentration range of target and is unlikely to gain accurate results [4,28]. Recently, smartphones have been widely used as photodetector in colorimetric analysis owing to their popularity and easy operation [29]. The fluorescent color of the sensors could be recorded by the camera of smartphone, and then was used as response signal for analyzing the target via the color analysis software installed in smartphone [28]. Moreover, the smartphone based fluorescent colorimetric detection could integrate with the 3D-printed subassemblies to achieve a portable device for the colorimetric detection of Cu2+.

Herein, OPD based CDs (Y-CDs) was hybridized with a blue fluorescence emission CDs (B-CDs) to facilely construct a dual emission fluorescence sensor for the ratiometric and colorimetric dual model detection of Cu2+ (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence intensity of Cu2+-sensitive emitter (Y-CDs) was significantly enhanced and then the Cu2+-insensitive emitter (B-CDs) was remarkably quenched by Förster resonance energy transfer. Furthermore, the sensor was integrated with smartphone and 3D-printed accessories to construct a smartphone-assisted sensing device for the colorimetric analysis of Cu2+. The internal referencing of ratiometric fluorescence sensor and its signal with interrelated opposite responses endow the above methods with more sensitive and reliable results. The dual model detection methods reported here exhibited satisfactory results for the determination of Cu2+ in real food samples, offering a prospective application for convenient and accurate analysis in practical application.

The Y-CDs were synthesized via microwave-assisted carbonization of o-phenylenediamine according to a previous report with a simple revision [30]. The B-CDs were prepared by hydrothermally treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine according to a published method with slight modifications [31]. The chemicals and apparatuses used in the experiment and the synthesis process of Y-CDs and B-CDs are elaborated in supplementary information (SI).

To improve the sensing performance of the multicolor fluorescence sensor for detecting Cu2+, the type and pH of buffer solution and reaction time were optimized. For the ratiometric fluorescent determination of Cu2+, the sensor was facilely constructed by direct hybridizing B-CDs and Y-CDs in HEPES buffer solution (10 mmol L−1, pH = 7.4) and the ratio of fluorescence intensity of F445 to F560 was adjusted to 7.5:1. And then 20 μL of Cu2+ with varying concentrations was added and incubated at 25°C for 30 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the sensor were recorded for analysis of Cu2+. The selectivity for Cu2+ detection was tested by substituting other interferential ions for Cu2+ in the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of Cu2+ with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: The smartphone-assisted sensing device was constructed by connecting a smartphone (Xiaomi 8) with 3D-printed accessories including sample slot, dark cavity and the holders of optical filter (400–700 nm) fabricated by a 3D printer (Senlang i3). Typically, various concentrations of Cu2+ were added into the above sensor solutions of the cuvettes. Then, the cuvettes were placed into the sample slot of device and the corresponding fluorescent images were captured by the smartphone (Fig. S1). The fluorescent pictures were processed by the application of Color Recognizer Application (Xiyi Technology Co., Ltd., Xiamen, China) installed on the smartphone to output the corresponding RGB values for analysis of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the proposed methods was evaluated by detecting Cu2+ in spiked samples (tea, kiwifruits and raisins) and the samples were pretreated with a digestion method (National food safety standard, determination of copper in food. GB 5009.13-2017, China). In detail, the real samples of a certain quality were mixed with the mixed solutions of 10 mL HNO3 and 0.5 mL HClO4 and placed at room temperature for 12 h. The sample solutions were heated until the solution became clear and then cooled to room temperature. Next, the sample solutions were diluted to 10 mL using deionized water. After adjusting the pH = 7.4, each sample was spiked with various concentrations of Cu2+ standard solutions and then detected by the proposed methods and the FAAS method.

As showed in Fig. 1 A, the Y-CDs possessed a good monodispersity with an average particle diameter of 3.83 ± 1.33 nm and lattice parameters of 0.18 nm. UV–vis spectrum of Y-CDs (Fig. 1B, blue line) displayed two typical absorption peaks around 230 and 290 nm, which were associated with the π–π* transition of CC and CN in the aromatic ring structure [18,25]. The fluorescent spectrum of Y-CDs presented an emission peak around 560 nm when excited at 365 nm (Fig. 1B, pink line) and the Y-CDs displayed a pale yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (the inset image). Furthermore, FT-IR spectrum of Y-CDs (Fig. S2A) indicated a characteristic broad band between 3000 and 3700 cm−1, which was related to the N–H stretching vibration [25]. The bands at 1638 and 1514 cm−1 were ascribed to the CO and N–H stretching vibration [18,25]. The absorption peaks at 1383 and 1139 cm−1 were accounted for C–N and C–O [32]. The other two characteristic peaks at 1583 and 1465 cm−1 were attributed to aromatic CC stretching frequency [14]. Moreover, in the XPS spectrum (Fig. S2B), the N1s were de-convoluted into three peaks at 399.5, 400.3 and 401.2 eV, which were corresponded to pyridine N, amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively [14,32]. The high-resolution spectra of C1s for Y-CDs was showed in Fig. S2C, and was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.7 eV (C–C/CC), 285.7 eV (C–N) and 288.7 eV (C–O) [32]. The findings of the XPS analysis were accord with that of FI-TR and indicated that a large amount of active groups containing N (e.g. –NH2) and aromatic structures existed on the surface of Y-CDs, providing potential recognition sites for coordinating Cu2+. Together with the findings of UV–vis spectrum, it can be concluded that the Y-CDs existed a large amount of residual groups of o-phenylenediamine, which may be originated from the incomplete carbonization of o-phenylenediamine during the synthesis process of Y-CDs [21,25]. The emission spectra of the Y-CDs at various excitation wavelengths were presented in Fig. 1C. The Y-CDs displayed a characteristic excitation-independent behavior and their emission peak at 560 nm without apparent shifts when excited from 300 to 500 nm.

As presented in Fig. 1D, the B-CDs were well-dispersed with the size distribution of 3.46–6.30 nm and lattice parameters of 0.24 nm. UV–vis spectrum exhibited two absorption peaks at ∼235 and 350 nm (Fig. 1E, blue line), which were associated with π−π* transition of CC and n−π* transition of C–N, respectively [33]. Fig. 1E indicated that the B-CDs possessed an emission peak at ∼445 nm when excited at 365 nm (pink line) and showed a bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (the inset image). FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs was displayed in Fig. S2A. The broad band of the B-CDs in the range of 3700–3000 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H [33]. The band at 2900 cm−1 was the alkyl stretch, which was the main component of the carbon network [34]. Moreover, the B-CDs also possessed a large amount of functional groups such as C–H (2947 cm−1), CO (1638 cm−1), CC (1552 cm−1), C–N (1384 cm−1) and C–O (1130 cm−1) [34,35]. Additionally, in the XPS spectrum (Fig. S2D), the high resolution spectrum of N1s possessed three contributions, corresponded to 400.9 eV for N–O, 400.2 eV for C–N and 399.4 eV for OC–NH [31,35]. The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S2E) of B-CDs at 287.7, 286.0 and 284.7 eV were ascribed to CO, C–N and C–C/CC, respectively. The analysis results by XPS were consistent with the findings of FT-IR analysis, which demonstrating the existence of amino and carboxyl groups [33]. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 1F, B-CDs exhibited an excitation-independent behavior that refer to no apparent shift of maximum fluorescence emission peak positions (445 nm) when excited at 300–400 nm.

The effect of different types of buffer solution on the sensor was firstly investigated in view of ΔF560/F445 (ΔF560/F445 means the changed value of the value of F560/F445 before and after introduction of Cu2+). As shown in Fig. S3A, ΔF560/F445 reached a maximum in HEPES buffer. Thus, the following experiments were conducted in HEPES buffer (10 mmol L−1). The influence of pH on the F560/F445 for sensor before and after introduction of Cu2+ was presented in Fig. S3B. F560/F445 did not obviously change before adding Cu2+ (p > 0.05) in the pH range from 6.4 to 8.2, while the ratio increased after adding Cu2+, which exhibited that the sensor possessed excellent pH stability and displayed more response sensitive with the increase of pH. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of Cu2+-sensitive emitter Y-CDs (F560) before and after the introduction of Cu2+ was studied when the pH was at the range of 6.4–8.2 and the results were shown in Fig. S3C. Before the introduction of Cu2+, F560 showed the pH-independent behavior (p > 0.05). However, in the presence of Cu2+, F560 increased obviously upon increasing pH from 6.4 to 7.4, while slowly decreased when the pH increased from 7.4 to 8.2, and reached a maximum value when pH = 7.4. In the weakly acidic media, the protonation of amino groups of Y-CDs inhibited the interaction between Y-CDs and Cu2+ [14,36]. The protonation process of amino was slowly taken over by the deprotonation process with the increase of pH, thus improved the interactions between Y-CDs and Cu2+. On the other hand, upon increasing pH over 7.4, the formation of compounds like Cu(OH)2 may occur because Cu2+ was more easily reacted with OH− in the sensor solution than Y-CDs [14]. Hence, pH = 7.4 was chosen for the optimal medium. Fig. S3D presented F560/F445 of sensor versus the different incubation time after addition of Cu2+. F560/F445 conspicuously increased with the reaction time of 0–30 min and then increased slightly after 30 min. Thus, the incubation time of 30 min was selected as the subsequent experiments. Therefore, the HEPES buffer solution (10 mmol L−1, pH = 7.4) and the incubation time of 30 min were selected as the optimum experimental conditions for the subsequent ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection experiments.

Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescent spectra of the sensor at different Cu2+ concentrations (0–45 μmol L−1) were shown in Fig. 2 A. Upon increasing Cu2+ concentration, F445 was dramatically decreased while F560 obviously increased. The ratio of F560/F445 vs Cu2+ concentration (0–45 μmol L−1) was showed in Fig. 2B. A linear fitting curve was described as F560/F445 = 0.0516 [C] + 0.1335 with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9964 ([C] was the concentration of Cu2+, μmol L−1). The detection limit (LOD) was ∼44.05 nmol L−1, based on S/N = 3. Compared with other fluorescent sensors (Table S1), the as-prepared CDs based fluorescence sensor possessed lower LOD and wider linear range. Furthermore, for the purpose of comparing the analytical performance of ratiometric fluorescence detection with the single emission detection, the analytical performance of Cu2+-sensitive emitter (Y-CDs) on the detection Cu2+ was also investigated. As presented in Fig. S4A and Fig. S4B, the Y-CDs based sensor possessed a good linear relationship in the Cu2+ concentrations of 0–25 μmol L−1 with a LOD of ∼146.41 mmol L−1 (S/N = 3), accompanying with a visual color change from pale yellow to yellow under a UV light. These results revealed that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor possessed a wider linear range and lower LOD. Moreover, with the increase of Cu2+ concentration in the ratiometric fluorescence sensor, an obvious fluorescent color changing from blue to yellow could be easily observed by naked eyes under a 365 nm UV light (the inset photograph in Fig. 2A), which matched well with the calculated chromaticity coordinates based on the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 2C). Thus, a linear relationship between the CIE coordinate and Cu2+ concentration can be obtained, which indicated that the sensor could be applied for the colorimetric detection of Cu2+ by analyzing the fluorescence color.

The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the sensor was investigated and the results were presented in Fig. 2D. The F560/F445 was immensely increased by Cu2+ (20 μmol L−1). However, no obviously change in F560/F445 was observed after adding Co2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Li+, Na+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Pb2+ and Al3+ (blue bars). Furthermore, the interfering metal ions also had no obvious impact on the F560/F445 of the sensor containing Cu2+ (pink bars). Moreover, as showed in Fig. 2D, the fluorescent color of the sensor remained blue after introducing the interferential ions, but the color was obviously changed from blue to yellow by Cu2+. These results indicated that this sensor can selectively detect Cu2+ and possess excellent anti-interference ability, which could be applied in practical sample detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of Cu2+ with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the inset of Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence color of the sensor at different Cu2+ concentrations (0–45 μmol L−1) were captured by a smartphone. Moreover, the values of B/G (blue and green channel) vs the concentration of Cu2+ was presented in Fig. 3A. As presented in Fig. 3B, an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9963) between the B/G and the concentration of Cu2+ (0–25 μmol L−1) was obtained. The linear equation was B/G = −0.063X + 2.026 with a LOD of 169.46 nmol L−1, (X was the concentration of Cu2+, μmol L−1). Although the LOD for colorimetric determination was higher than that of ratiometric fluorescence detection, the colorimetric detection displayed a comparable liner range and LOD compared with previous colorimetric detection methods (Table S2). The results of Cu2+ colorimetric detection demonstrated that this device possessed a rapid (30 min) and easy readouts for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the proposed methods was evaluated by the determination of Cu2+ in real samples. As presented in Table 1 , satisfied results of the spiked food samples with recoveries of 96.8–105.9% and RSD <3.8% were achieved by ratiometric fluorescence detection. Furthermore, the recovery rate of Cu2+ determination by colorimetric detection were depicted in Table 1, recoveries of 94.4–108.5% with RSD <7.7% were gained in the same spiked samples. The results indicated that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor and the platform have promising application for the determination of Cu2+ in real food samples. Furthermore, the reliability of the proposed methods was verified by using the FAAS method to validate the analysis results gained by the platform. As presented in Table S3, the detected results by FAAS were accord with the result obtained by the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection (p > 0.05), indicating that the proposed methods could be used for the detection of Cu2+ in real samples.

With the respective introduction of Cu2+ (20 μmol L−1) to Y-CDs and to B-CDs, the F560 of Y-CDs was enhanced while the F445 of B-CDs remained almost unchanged (Fig. 4 A). However, upon addition of Cu2+ (20 μmol L−1) into the hybrid of Y-CDs and B-CDs, the F560 of Y-CDs in the mixture was enhanced obviously while the F445 of B-CDs was quenched significantly (refer to Fig. 2A). The fluorescence enhancement mechanism of Y-CDs by Cu2+ was firstly studied. Fig. 4B exhibited the UV–vis absorption spectra of Y-CDs with various concentrations of Cu2+. A new absorbance peak around ∼420 nm was formed and its absorption gradually increased upon introduction of Cu2+, accompanying with a visual color change from pale yellow to orange under ambient light (the inset of Fig. 4B). The significant changes in the UV spectra of Y-CDs toward different concentrations of Cu2+ verified direct binding between Cu2+ and Y-CDs in the ground state. The new absorption peak could be assigned to the formation of Cu2+-Y-CDs complexes of Cu2+ with the aromatic amino groups of the residual ligands of o-phenylenediamine [18]. Therefore, the predominant enhanced mechanism was the inhibition of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) by forming the complex on the Y-CDs [14,18].

Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-CDs after adding Cu2+ in the sensor was studied. As presented in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence emission spectrum of B-CDs overlapped with the UV–vis absorption peak of Cu2+-Y-CDs, thus Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) could occur from the B-CDs to the complex, leading to fluorescence quenching of B-CDs [37–40]. Moreover, the non-radiative energy transfer could substantially change the exciton lifetime of the donor, thus the fluorescence lifetime could be used as another proof to demonstrate the FRET process [41]. As showed in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs in the sensor decreased from 15.37 ns to 9.63 ns after adding Cu2+. Hence, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the B-CDs after adding Cu2+ in the sensor was attributed to the FRET process.

In this work, a multicolor fluorescence sensor has been facilely constructed for ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of Cu2+ through the combination of two kinds of CDs with different emission. After introducing Cu2+ into the sensor, F560 of Y-CDs was enhanced while F445 of B-CDs was quenched. The unique interrelated opposite response makes the fluorescent color of the sensor changing obviously from blue to yellow under a 365 nm UV light. The special ratiometric fluorescence response induced by Cu2+ endowed the sensor with excellent selectivity and reliability for detecting Cu2+. Furthermore, the portable smartphone-assisted platform has been constructed and successfully applied to the colorimetric detection of Cu2+ in real samples, which provided a promising tool for the convenient and accurate detection of Cu2+ in the food to ensure food safety and improve human health.

Gengli Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Xueli Luo: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Wanmei Lin: Software, Supervision. Wenzhi Tang: Resources. Tianli Yue: Supervision. Jianlong Wang: Resources. Zhonghong Li: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 32172327] and the Social Development Grant of Shaanxi Province (2018SF-401).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: COX16 is required for assembly of cytochrome c oxidase in human cells and is involved in copper delivery to COX2

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semi-quantitative and visual assay of copper ions by fluorescent test paper constructed with dual-emission carbon dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A turn-on red-emitting fluorescent probe for determination of copper(II) ions in food samples and living zebrafish
